note: `overlapping` is the array of overlapping env-key strings. SPEC §5 maps empty/non-empty branching to `A.match`; `.length > 0` is a property check (not in the §3 method list), so converting to `A.isNonEmptyReadonlyArray`/`A.match` is a non-mechanical control-flow rewrite. Human confirms intended shape.

`hooks` is an array of hook entries; early-return guard when empty. SPEC §5 maps empty branching to `A.match`/`A.isEmptyReadonlyArray`, but `.length === 0` early-return guards do not translate mechanically. Human confirms.

Compound empty-array guard on two arrays (`hostHooks`, `sandboxHooks`). SPEC §5 maps empty branching to `A.match`/`A.isEmptyReadonlyArray`, but a compound short-circuit guard is a structural rewrite, not a direct swap. Human confirms.
`paths` is an array; early-return guard when empty. SPEC §5 maps empty branching to `A.match`/`A.isEmptyReadonlyArray`; `.length === 0` guard is a non-mechanical rewrite. Human confirms.

`commands[0]` numeric index access on the `ReadonlyArray<string>` `commands` inside a `commands.length === 1 ? ... : ...` ternary. SPEC §3 maps index access to `A.get`/`A.head`/`A.last` and empty/single branching to `A.match`. The `.length === 1` check plus index access is a single-element branch best expressed via `A.match`/`A.get` returning an `Option`, which changes the surrounding shape. Human confirms whether to rewrite as `A.match`.

`remaining` is the array returned by `buildRemainingCommands`; non-empty guard appending lines. SPEC §5 maps non-empty branching to `A.match`/`A.isNonEmptyReadonlyArray`; the guarded body uses imperative `A.appendInPlace`, so a mechanical swap is not clearly better. Human confirms.
Second `remaining.length > 0` non-empty guard (else-branch of the same function). Same considerations as line 190. Human confirms.

`remainingPatches` is an `Array<string>` (typed via `as Array<string>`); empty guard removing a directory. SPEC §5 maps empty branching to `A.match`/`A.isEmptyReadonlyArray`; non-mechanical rewrite. Human confirms.
`remainingSandcastleEntries` is an `Array<string>`; empty guard removing a directory. SPEC §5 maps empty branching to `A.match`/`A.isEmptyReadonlyArray`; non-mechanical rewrite. Human confirms.

`untrackedFiles` is an array (`parseNulSeparatedPaths(...)` or `[]`); boolean assignment of non-emptiness. Mechanically replaceable with `A.isNonEmptyReadonlyArray(untrackedFiles)`. Flagged needsHumanDecision because `.length` property checks were marked borderline in the existing inventory (see beep__acp/Array.json).
`nonEmptyPatches` is an array; non-empty guard running `git am --abort`. SPEC §5 maps non-empty branching to `A.match`/`A.isNonEmptyReadonlyArray`. Human confirms.

`nonEmptyPatches.length > 0` used as the `hasCommits` boolean field of a `RecoveryInput`. Mechanically replaceable with `A.isNonEmptyReadonlyArray(nonEmptyPatches)`. Flagged needsHumanDecision per the borderline-`.length` convention.

Second `nonEmptyPatches.length > 0` used as the `hasCommits` field of a `SyncOutResult`. Mechanically replaceable with `A.isNonEmptyReadonlyArray(nonEmptyPatches)`. Flagged needsHumanDecision per the borderline-`.length` convention.

`texts` is a mutable local accumulator (`A.appendInPlace` + `texts.length = 0` reset on line 568) used in a hot stream-parsing loop. The `.length > 0` flush guard is part of an imperative accumulation pattern that SPEC §5 says to leave as justified-local mutable usage; rewriting to `A.match` would fight the accumulation idiom. Human confirms whether to keep imperative or restructure.
Trailing flush guard on the same mutable `texts` accumulator as line 561 (post-loop). Same imperative-accumulation considerations. Human confirms.

Ternary on `texts` (mutable accumulator) non-emptiness selecting a final parsed event. SPEC §5 maps this to `A.match`/`A.isNonEmptyReadonlyArray`, but `texts` is built via `A.appendInPlace` in a loop. Human confirms shape.

nativeSymbol: Array index access (RegExpMatchArray capture group match[1])
suggestedTarget: A.get(match, 1) (returns Option) — or keep as regex capture-group access
note: `match` is a `RegExpMatchArray` element from `[...Str.matchAll(PLACEHOLDER_PATTERN)(prompt)]`; `match[1]` reads capture group 1. Per disambiguation, RegExp-related access is borderline — capture-group index access is not idiomatic array-data access. `A.get(match, 1)` would yield an `Option` and change the `undefined` handling on the next line. Human decides whether this counts as an Array violation.
